html:
D-BOX Technologies (DBOXF) at $0.65

D-BOX is no longer just a niche motion-seat hardware vendor. The cleaner thesis is that it is becoming a small haptics toll-road: sell the installed system once, then collect higher-margin rights, rental, maintenance, and royalty revenue as more D-BOX-enabled screens, venues, simulators, games, and coded content get used.

The market still sees movie-theater capex cyclicality. The filings now show an inflection. Through the first nine months of fiscal 2026, revenue was C$42.9M, up 26%, already exceeding full-year fiscal 2025 revenue. Adjusted EBITDA was C$12.1M, up 112%, with a 28% margin. Operating cash flow was C$10.1M. Cash was C$16.2M against only C$0.4M of debt. The reported C$15.5M net profit includes a C$6.4M deferred tax asset, so it should not be blindly annualized, but the pretax operating progress is real.

The key line is not hardware sales. It is “rights for use, rental and maintenance,” which rose 31% year over year in the first nine months to C$11.6M. That is the recurring layer. In Q1 and Q2, royalty revenue hit records of C$4.0M and C$4.5M. Q3 dipped to C$3.1M because the North American box office was soft and lacked enough premium-format tentpoles, but active screens still rose 12.8% year over year to 1,135. The installed base expanded even when the box office was choppy.

That is the variant perception: D-BOX is not a bet on one blockbuster quarter. It is a bet that premium theatrical formats keep taking share of moviegoing dollars, and that D-BOX’s expanding installed base converts each strong release slate into high-margin revenue without proportionate cost growth.

Cinemark is the most important validation point. In 2025, Cinemark agreed to add D-BOX haptic recliner seats in more than 70 additional U.S. auditoriums over 18 months, after adding 50 in 2024, plus 14 in Argentina. That matters because exhibitors do not add this product for novelty. They add it because premium seating increases monetization per patron and can keep demand alive after opening weekend. D-BOX also deactivated 35 dormant international screens in Q3, which optically reduces net adds but improves the quality of the royalty base.

The prior bullish narrative around an undisclosed Logitech royalty stream should not be the center of the thesis. The stronger case is based on disclosed economics: theatrical royalties, B2B sim-racing integrations, FIA licensing, Playseat and other sim partners, F1 Arcade exposure, and a software/content library of more than 3,000 coded titles. Retail and social chatter is useful here because users are noticing D-BOX in sim racing and Star Citizen haptics, but they also complain that full systems cost thousands of dollars. That supports management’s strategic pivot away from low-margin direct-to-consumer hardware and toward partners, venues, commercial centers, and embedded integrations where hardware cost is amortized across usage.

The balance sheet and capital allocation have also changed. D-BOX authorized a normal course issuer bid for up to 20.96M shares, about 10% of the public float, running from March 27, 2026 to March 26, 2027. A microcap with C$16M of cash, almost no debt, positive operating cash flow, and a buyback is a different animal from the dilution-prone D-BOX of the past. The buyback can offset option dilution and force per-share earnings growth if cash generation continues.

Valuation is no longer optically dirt cheap, but it is still cheap if the royalty mix keeps rising. At roughly $0.65, the market cap is about $144M, or around C$198M. Net of last disclosed cash, enterprise value is roughly C$182M. If fiscal 2026 lands near C$55M of revenue and C$15M of adjusted EBITDA, the stock is around 12x current-year EBITDA. That looks full for hardware, but not for a capital-light royalty and IP platform still compounding screens, content, and partner channels. On fiscal 2027 economics, C$62M to C$68M of revenue and C$19M to C$22M of adjusted EBITDA is plausible if theatrical rollouts continue and royalties normalize with a better release slate. That puts the stock closer to 8x to 10x forward EBITDA after net cash and buybacks.

The biggest risk is that the market decides this is still just a tiny hardware company. Q3 proved royalties can fall when the box office is weak. Sim racing revenue is growing slowly, simulation and training is lumpy, OTC liquidity is poor, and the stock has already run hard from its lows. Competition from 4DX, cheaper motion systems, bass shakers, and consumer haptic workarounds is real. But D-BOX has patents, studio relationships, installed infrastructure, content depth, and now operating leverage. Those are not easy to replicate at this size.

My view: DBOXF is a high-upside microcap platform transition where the financial statements have started to confirm the story. The stock is not undiscovered by price action, but it remains under-owned, thinly followed, and misunderstood as a theater gimmick rather than a recurring haptics layer.

Bear target: $0.35, down 46%. The box office weakens, exhibitors pause rollouts, royalties stall near C$11M to C$13M annually, EBITDA falls back toward C$6M to C$8M, and the stock de-rates to a small hardware multiple.

Base target: $1.10, up 69%. Revenue grows into the mid-C$60M range, adjusted EBITDA approaches C$20M, buybacks reduce the share count modestly, and the market values D-BOX at a mid-teens multiple on forward EBITDA as recurring revenue becomes more visible.

Bull target: $2.30, up 254%. The theatrical royalty base compounds, Cinemark-style deployments broaden, sim racing centers and location-based entertainment scale, gaming integrations like Star Citizen prove demand for D-BOX-coded haptics outside theaters, and EBITDA reaches C$30M-plus with a platform multiple.
